tossups.question: Geremie Barme suggested that this play is a reinterpretation of The Passerby, which preceded every production of this play’s original run. The script of this play refers to the actors with letters A through G in a passage in which they all comment on this play’s plot at once in “polyphonic dialogue.” The author recommends a leitmotif representing a “Silent Man” be used throughout this play, some of whose characters talk of wanting to taste yogurt, play a (*) chess champion, or take an entrance exam. Early in this play, Hothead fights Glasses, whose watch later indicates that ten years have passed while they’ve been waiting. For 10 points, no one is able to get a ride to the city from the title location of what play by Gao Xingjian [“GOW shing-JYAN”]?
tossups.question_sanitized: Geremie Barme suggested that this play is a reinterpretation of The Passerby, which preceded every production of this play's original run. The script of this play refers to the actors with letters A through G in a passage in which they all comment on this play's plot at once in "polyphonic dialogue." The author recommends a leitmotif representing a "Silent Man" be used throughout this play, some of whose characters talk of wanting to taste yogurt, play a (*) chess champion, or take an entrance exam. Early in this play, Hothead fights Glasses, whose watch later indicates that ten years have passed while they've been waiting. For 10 points, no one is able to get a ride to the city from the title location of what play by Gao Xingjian ["GOW shing-JYAN"]?
tossups.answer: The Bus Stop [or Che zhan] (The Passerby is by Lu Xun)
tossups.answer_sanitized: The Bus Stop [or Che zhan] (The Passerby is by Lu Xun)

tossups.question: This author developed his idea of a “symbol of angst” from his translation of Kuriyagawa Hakuson’s Symbolism of Depression. This author noted “dreams are fine, otherwise money is essential” in an essay that expands on the meaning of a play’s German title, Ein Puppenheim. This author recounted leaving medical school after watching a war film in a preface that compares his society to sleepers in an “iron house without windows.” This author, who coined the name of the (*) “gods and demons” fantasy genre, gave a speech at a woman’s college about Nora leaving home at the end of A Doll’s House. A character created by this author tries to sign his name as a perfect circle prior to his execution in a story about his “spiritual victories.” The collection Call to Arms is by, for 10 points, what author of The True Story of Ah Q?
tossups.question_sanitized: This author developed his idea of a "symbol of angst" from his translation of Kuriyagawa Hakuson's Symbolism of Depression. This author noted "dreams are fine, otherwise money is essential" in an essay that expands on the meaning of a play's German title, Ein Puppenheim. This author recounted leaving medical school after watching a war film in a preface that compares his society to sleepers in an "iron house without windows." This author, who coined the name of the (*) "gods and demons" fantasy genre, gave a speech at a woman's college about Nora leaving home at the end of A Doll's House. A character created by this author tries to sign his name as a perfect circle prior to his execution in a story about his "spiritual victories." The collection Call to Arms is by, for 10 points, what author of The True Story of Ah Q?
tossups.answer: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsün; or Zhou Shuren; or Zhou Zhangshou]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsun; or Zhou Shuren; or Zhou Zhangshou]

tossups.question: A book in this language gives metaphors for spontaneous emotion and proper structure to find the “pivot of literature.” That text in this language analogizes the “literary mind” to the metaphorical “carving” of animals. The dictum “poetry expresses what is intended in the mind” appears in the “Great Preface” to a collection in this language, which Stephen Owen’s book on its “literary thought” collects with a text translated as “Rhymeprose” or “Essay on Literature.” A “brief history” of this language’s fiction coined the genre of “gods and demons” and excerpts a collection of “strange tales” from a “studio.” An early collection in this language comprises 305 “Airs of the States,” odes, and hymns. The study of one novel in this language is known as Redology. For 10 points, the Classic of Poetry is among what language’s Five Classics?
tossups.question_sanitized: A book in this language gives metaphors for spontaneous emotion and proper structure to find the "pivot of literature." That text in this language analogizes the "literary mind" to the metaphorical "carving" of animals. The dictum "poetry expresses what is intended in the mind" appears in the "Great Preface" to a collection in this language, which Stephen Owen's book on its "literary thought" collects with a text translated as "Rhymeprose" or "Essay on Literature." A "brief history" of this language's fiction coined the genre of "gods and demons" and excerpts a collection of "strange tales" from a "studio." An early collection in this language comprises 305 "Airs of the States," odes, and hymns. The study of one novel in this language is known as Redology. For 10 points, the Classic of Poetry is among what language's Five Classics?
tossups.answer: Chinese [accept wényán or Mandarin; accept Readings in Chinese Literary Thought or A Brief History of Chinese Fiction] (The first line refers to feng and gu, or “wind” and “bone.” The works are The Literary Mind and the Carving of Dragons by Liu Xie and Wen Fu by Lu Ji. A Brief History of Chinese Fiction is by Lu Xun. Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio is by Pu Songling.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Chinese [accept wenyan or Mandarin; accept Readings in Chinese Literary Thought or A Brief History of Chinese Fiction] (The first line refers to feng and gu, or "wind" and "bone." The works are The Literary Mind and the Carving of Dragons by Liu Xie and Wen Fu by Lu Ji. A Brief History of Chinese Fiction is by Lu Xun. Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio is by Pu Songling.)

tossups.question: A character created by this author smashes her forehead against a table during her forced second marriage. In that story by this author, a woman who asks the narrator questions about the afterlife is metaphorically called the “New Year’s Sacrifice.” A tea shop owner in a story by this author uses profits to buy a folk cure to unsuccessfully save their son from tuberculosis, the title “Medicine.” A man who hasn't seen the moon in 30 years thinks his brother (*) killed his sister in a story by this author that ends with the line, “Save the children…” That story by this author depicts a character who reads “Eat People” in a book and was inspired by a Gogol story of the same title. The collection Call to Arms is by, for 10 points, what Chinese author of the short story “Diary of a Madman”?
tossups.question_sanitized: A character created by this author smashes her forehead against a table during her forced second marriage. In that story by this author, a woman who asks the narrator questions about the afterlife is metaphorically called the "New Year's Sacrifice." A tea shop owner in a story by this author uses profits to buy a folk cure to unsuccessfully save their son from tuberculosis, the title "Medicine." A man who hasn't seen the moon in 30 years thinks his brother (*) killed his sister in a story by this author that ends with the line, "Save the children..." That story by this author depicts a character who reads "Eat People" in a book and was inspired by a Gogol story of the same title. The collection Call to Arms is by, for 10 points, what Chinese author of the short story "Diary of a Madman"?

tossups.question: A character created by this author writes about “A Happy Family” living in the city “A” as he discusses the price of the firewood with his wife. A preface to one collection by this author likens society to an “iron house without windows” in which people will die of suffocation in their sleep. A story by this author depicts a wreath of red and white flowers on the grave of a revolutionary in whose blood a bread roll is dipped to serve as a quack remedy. A scholarly character created by this author (*) crawls toan inn on his hands after his legs are broken for stealing.Another of this author’s characters fails to draw a perfect circle prior to his execution, and claims “spiritual victories” despite being constantly humiliated. For 10 points, name this author whose collection A Call to Arms contains the stories Medicine, Kong Yiji (“kong yee-jee”) and The True Story of Ah Q (“ah cue”).
tossups.question_sanitized: A character created by this author writes about "A Happy Family" living in the city "A" as he discusses the price of the firewood with his wife. A preface to one collection by this author likens society to an "iron house without windows" in which people will die of suffocation in their sleep. A story by this author depicts a wreath of red and white flowers on the grave of a revolutionary in whose blood a bread roll is dipped to serve as a quack remedy. A scholarly character created by this author (*) crawls toan inn on his hands after his legs are broken for stealing.Another of this author's characters fails to draw a perfect circle prior to his execution, and claims "spiritual victories" despite being constantly humiliated. For 10 points, name this author whose collection A Call to Arms contains the stories Medicine, Kong Yiji ("kong yee-jee") and The True Story of Ah Q ("ah cue").

tossups.question: In one story by this author, one character taboos words such as “ringworm”, “lamp” and “candle” and flies into a rage whenever accidentally using them. In another work by this author, the narrator hears of a man’s heart and liver being eaten after being beaten to death in the Wolf Cub village and ends (*) “Save the children!” In one story by this author, the title character loses his pigtail after it is cut off by a “Foreign Imitation Devil.” That character created by this author slaps himself after losing fights to justify them as “spiritual victories.”For 10 points, name this Chinese author of A Madman’s Diary and The True Story of Ah Q.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one story by this author, one character taboos words such as "ringworm", "lamp" and "candle" and flies into a rage whenever accidentally using them. In another work by this author, the narrator hears of a man's heart and liver being eaten after being beaten to death in the Wolf Cub village and ends (*) "Save the children!" In one story by this author, the title character loses his pigtail after it is cut off by a "Foreign Imitation Devil." That character created by this author slaps himself after losing fights to justify them as "spiritual victories."For 10 points, name this Chinese author of A Madman's Diary and The True Story of Ah Q.

tossups.question: One story by this author centers on a woman who tells the story of how her son was eaten by wolves before dying on the day of the title “New Year’s Sacrifice.” The title object of a story by this author is a loaf of bread dipped in blood. A character created by this author dislikes how townspeople call a “long bench” a “straight bench” and has a ringworm scar on his head. This author of “Medicine” described a man who drags himself by his hands to a tavern to order wine and collected many of his most famous stories in (*) Call to Arms. This man satirized his culture with a protagonist who signs his name as a circle and constantly claims “spiritual victories” in one story, while in another, the central character sees the command “eat people” written in between the lines of books. For 10 points, name this Chinese author of stories such as “Diary of a Madman” and “The True Story of Ah Q.”
tossups.answer: Lu Xun [accept Lu Hsün; accept Zhou Shuren] (The unnamed story is “Kong Yiji.”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [accept  Lu Hsun; accept  Zhou Shuren] (The unnamed story is "Kong Yiji.")

For 10 points, name this Chinese author of stories such as "Diary of a Madman" and "The True Story of Ah Q."

tossups.question: In a story by this author, an old man tries to use a bread roll dipped in an executed revolutionary’s blood to cure his son’s illness. The title character of a novella by this author steals turnips from the Convent of Quiet Self-Improvement. This author of “Medicine” created a character who provokes Whiskers Wang (“wong”) into beating him in a fight; that character created by this author is unable to sign his name as a perfect circle or recite lines from Chinese opera before his execution. This author depicted a peasant who claims “spiritual victories” and a narrator who imagines that texts contain the words “Eat People!” in two works within the collection Call to Arms. For 10 points, name this Chinese author of The True Story of Ah Q and “A Madman’s Diary.”

packet.name: D_ Penn State A, Cornell A, Harvard A, MIT C

tossups.question: This author advocated “[worshipping] Darwin and Ibsen” and praised Ibsen’s “all or nothing” approach in a “random thought” later collected in the book Jottings Under Lamplight. A speech by this author discussed the “compulsion” of a character for whom “Dreams are fine, but otherwise money is essential.” In a preface, this writer described abandoning medical school to treat the spirit of his country’s people, who are imagined as sleepers fated to painlessly suffocate within an (*) “iron house without windows.” This writer coined the “gods and demons” genre to describe fantasy novels like The Investiture of the Gods in a 1930 “Brief History” of his country’s fiction. This author, who lectured at a women’s college on “What Happens After Nora Leaves Home?”, wrote in vernacular for a collection of stories aligned with the May Fourth movement. For 10 points, name this Chinese critic whose collection Call to Arms contains “A Madman’s Diary.”
tossups.answer: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsün; or Zhou Shuren] (The third sentence describes the preface to Call to Arms.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsun; or Zhou Shuren] (The third sentence describes the preface to Call to Arms.)

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a small child asking for food is hit on the head by her mother and told to “Stop that noise! Little slut,” prompting the child’s great-grandmother to repeat the line, “Each generation is worse than the last.” In a story by this author, a guard referred to as “Red-eye” beats up a prisoner whose blood is mixed with a loaf of bread eaten by the protagonist’s tubercular son. Mr. Sevenpounder is threatened with execution under a new political regime analogized to a (*) “storm” in a story by this author set in a town compared to a “teacup.” At the end of a novella by this author, a crowd is disappointed when the protagonist doesn’t sing any lines from an opera while he is taken to be executed. The title character of that novella by this author of “Medicine” tries to draw a perfect circle instead of signing his name and rationalizes his defeats as “spiritual victories.” For 10 points, The True Story of Ah Q was written by what Chinese author?
tossups.answer: Lu Xun [or Lu Sun; or Zhou Shuren; or Zhou Zhangshou]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Sun; or Zhou Shuren; or Zhou Zhangshou]

tossups.question: In an early manifesto, this writer argued that his country’s literature needed “demonic” “Mara poets” in the mold of Lord Byron. This author initiated the Modern Woodcut Movement by organizing a workshop led by Kakichi Uchiyama. Those woodcuts illustrated many of his works, including a late sequence of 23 prose poems titled Wild Grass. This writer dropped out of medical school after seeing a slideshow in which apathetic onlookers watched the beheading of a prisoner of war. This founder of the League of Left-Wing Writers asked whether it was just to wake a few people up only for them to suffocate in a parable about sleepers in an iron house. That argument prefaces a collection by this author, which includes a story whose narrator sees the words “Eat people!” appear in the Confucian classics. For 10 points, name this Chinese modernist author of A Call to Arms, which contains “A Madman’s Diary.”

That argument prefaces a collection by this author, which includes a story whose narrator sees the words "Eat people!" appear in the Confucian classics. For 10 points, name this Chinese modernist author of A Call to Arms, which contains "A Madman's Diary."

tossups.question: Osamu Dazai's novel Sekibetsu fictionalizes this man's time at medical school, which this man also wrote about in "Mr. Fujino." This man argued in a lecture given at a women's college that due to her inability to support herself, Nora Helmer will be forced to return home. After having his legs broken for stealing, a scholarly character created by this man crawls to a tavern and orders (*) wine. In one story by this author, the narrator feels disgusted eating fish because it reminds him of villagers of the Wolf Cub village. In that story by this author, the narrator, who suspects his brother fed him his sister's flesh, says "save the children…" because he believes everyone is a cannibal. For 10 points, what early proponent of vernacular Chinese wrote "Kong Yiji" and "A Madman's Diary"?
tossups.answer_sanitized: Lu Xun [or Lu Hsun; or Zhou Shuren]

In a short story by this author, English-speaking philosophers who survived the Great Flood are supplied by flying machines from the Land of Clever Tricks. A character created by this author worries about the consequences of stepping on the Records of the Past after imagining that a (*) dog looked at him strangely. Delusions about the residents of Wolf Cub Village lead that character by this author to begin seeing the words “eat people!” written in the margins of classical texts. For 10 points, name this Chinese author of stories like “Bringing Back the Dead,” “Taming the Flood,” and “Diary of a Madman.” A

tossups.question: The preface to one work by this man discusses whether it's a good idea to wake up people sleeping in an inescapable iron house if they'd feel no pain when they die in their sleep. That story, “Storm in a Teacup” appeared with works like “Medicine” in this man's collection Call to Arms. For 10 points, name this Chinese author of The True Story of Ah Q and A Madman's Diary, the leading author of the May Fourth Movement.

This theatrical movement fathered by Ibsen inspired Chinese huàjù, or “spoken drama,” in contrast to traditional sung theatre. During the Cultural Revolution, the CCP mandated this style’s “socialist” form.

In two nods to Ibsen’s Ghosts, this landmark huàjù play by Cáo Yú (“tsao yoo”) has a retrospective prologue and epilogue set in a Catholic hospital, and ends by revealing that Zhou Ping’s relationship with the servant Sifeng was incestuous.

parts: Identify this language, many of whose modern poets use the eight-syllable metrical line from an epic poem whose hero wins his kingdom by riding his magical steed to victory in a horse race.

Tsering Dondrup’s story about a lazy everyman, “Ralo,” prompted an examination of Tibetan national character similar to the one in China occasioned by this novella, whose protagonist rationalizes all of his mishaps as “spiritual victories.”

Another modern Tibetan author, Jamyang Norbu, is best known for a novel in which Sherlock Holmes joins forces with Hurree Babu, a spy who directly supervises the title orphan of this Rudyard Kipling novel.

The True Story of Ah Q [or Ā Q Zhèngzhuàn] (by Lu Xun)

The True Story of Ah-Q or AhQ Zhenzhuan
Mao Dun or Mao Tun or Shen Dehong or Shen Yanbing
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The first work in his first short story collection was named for a Gogol work. For 10 points:
parts_sanitized: Name this modern Chinese writer who wrote about a man who believes the people of his village, including Mr. Chao and his own elder brother are cannibals in "A Madman's Diary" from his Call to Arms.
The longest section of Lu Xun's Call to Arms is devoted to this work where the protagonist is a liar and laughingstock who executed during the Xinhai Revolution after failing to join in.
Lu Xun published many works in the "New Wave of Novels" column edited by this man. The first PRC Culture Minister, he is also known for writing Spring Silkworms and Midnight.

bonuses.leadin: In the often-printed preface to this work, the author claims he's been unable to erase these stories from his memory, and relates his youthful travels to a pawnbroker and a medicine shop. FTPE:
parts: Name this 1922 short story collection which contains "A Madman's Diary," and ”The True Story of Ah Q.” It was the first collection by its author, released before Wandering.
Of course, all of those works were written by this 20th century Chinese author who also wrote "Storm in a Teacup."

First published in New Youth magazine, this story in Call to Arms describes a title scholar who is impoverished because he cannot pass the Imperial examination, and resorts to thievery while copying manuscripts.
answers_sanitized: Call to Arms [or Na Han or Cry Out]
Lu Xun [or Zhou Shuren]
"Kong Yiji"
